questions.question: How can you design a solution to transfer log data from your set of YouTube channels into Google Cloud for analysis? Your design should allow global marketing teams to perform ANSI SQL and other types of analysis on the latest YouTube channel log data.
options: Use Storage Transfer Service to transfer the offsite backup files to a Cloud Storage Multi-Regional storage bucket as a final destination
Use Storage Transfer Service to transfer the offsite backup files to a Cloud Storage Regional bucket as a final destination.
Use BigQuery Data Transfer Service to transfer the offsite backup files to a Cloud Storage Multi-Regional storage bucket as a final destination.
Use BigQuery Data Transfer Service to transfer the offsite backup files to a Cloud Storage Regional storage bucket as a final destination
answer: Use Storage Transfer Service to transfer the offsite backup files to a Cloud Storage Multi-Regional storage bucket as a final destination
questions.explanation: Correct Option A. Use Storage Transfer Service to transfer the offsite backup files to a Cloud Storage Multi‑Regional storage bucket as a final destination Correct because the Storage Transfer Service is designed to move large volumes of log or backup data into Cloud Storage. Using a Multi‑Regional bucket ensures global availability and durability, which is ideal for marketing teams spread across different regions. Once in Cloud Storage, the data can be loaded into BigQuery for ANSI SQL analysis. This design minimizes complexity and ensures scalability. Incorrect Option B. Regional bucket with Storage Transfer Service Incorrect because a Regional bucket restricts data availability to a single region. Since the requirement is for global marketing teams, Multi‑Regional storage is more appropriate. Option C. BigQuery Data Transfer Service to Cloud Storage Multi‑Regional bucket Incorrect because the BigQuery Data Transfer Service (DTS) is used to transfer data directly into BigQuery, not into Cloud Storage. DTS is not designed for moving raw log backups into Cloud Storage. Option D. BigQuery Data Transfer Service to Cloud Storage Regional bucket Incorrect for the same reason as Option C: DTS does not transfer data into Cloud Storage. Additionally, using a Regional bucket would not meet the global availability requirement.

questions.question: A team of researchers is running a high performance distributed computing platform on premises but wants to migrate to Google Cloud. The platform uses virtual machines. The researchers want to be able to scale up the number of virtual machines in the cluster based on CPU load. What would you recommend they use?
options: Kubernetes cluster
Managed instance groups
Unmanaged instance groups
Cloud Run
answer: Managed instance groups
questions.explanation: The correct answer is managed instance groups, which is a way of deploying Compute Engine instances basesed on a template. Kubernetes is used for running containers, not virtual machines. Cloud Run is also used to run containers not virtual machines. Unmanaged instance groups run virtual machines but do not support autoscaling. See https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instance-groups

questions.question: You have been tasked with ensuring the successful transfer of 100 TB of data from the AWS S3 object storage system. This is a one time transfer. A complete and reliable transfer of all data is a top priority. How would you recommend loading this data into Cloud Storage?
options: gsutil cp
Cloud Dataflow
Cloud Storage Transfer Service
Transfer Appliance
answer: Cloud Storage Transfer Service
questions.explanation: Correct Option C – Cloud Storage Transfer Service This is the recommended solution for a large, one‑time transfer (100 TB) from AWS S3 to Google Cloud Storage. It is designed for reliability, scale, and completeness, ensuring all data is transferred successfully. Provides built‑in error handling, retry logic, and monitoring, which are critical for such a large migration. Incorrect Option A – gsutil cp Suitable for small or ad‑hoc transfers. Not recommended for 100 TB because it lacks the reliability and scalability needed for such a massive dataset. It would be error‑prone and inefficient. Option B – Cloud Dataflow Dataflow is for data processing pipelines, not bulk transfer. It can transform and stream data, but it is not intended for direct large‑scale migration from S3 to Cloud Storage. Option D – Transfer Appliance Transfer Appliance is used when moving petabytes of data from on‑premises to Google Cloud. In this case, the source is AWS S3 (already in the cloud), so Transfer Appliance is unnecessary and incorrect.

questions.question: A group of analysts is migrating a Hadoop cluster from on premises to GCP. They want to follow Google Cloud recommended best practices. What should they do as part of the migration?
options: Use ephemeral clusters and Cloud Storage instead of HDFS on local storage.
Use ephemeral clusters and use HDFS on local storage.
Continually run clusters and use Cloud Storage instead of HDFS on local storage.
Continually run clusters and use HDFS on local storage.
answer: Use ephemeral clusters and Cloud Storage instead of HDFS on local storage.
questions.explanation: Google Cloud recommends using ephemeral clusters. Since clusters start quickly you do not need to keep clusters running to avoid long startup time. Google Cloud also recommends using Cloud Storage to store persistent data so data does not have to be copied from a cluster before shutting down and then copied back to a cluster when starting a new cluster. https://cloud.google.com/blog/topics/developers-practitioners/dataproc-best-practices-guide

questions.question: Your company is migrating an on-premises long-term storage archive to Google Cloud. The archived files are accessed on average about once every 30 days. You would like to minimize the cost of storage. What storage option would you recommend?
options: Nearline Storage
Coldline Storage
Multi-regional storage
Persistent Disks
answer: Nearline Storage
questions.explanation: Nearline Storage is a class of Cloud Storage designed for objects that will be accessed at most once every 30 days. Coldline Storage is suitable for objects accessed at most once per 90 days. Multi-regional storage is best suited for objects that should have low latency access from multiple regions. Persistent disks are used with Compute Engine instances and Kubernetes Engine clusters and should not be used for archival storage. See https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/storage-classes

questions.question: You have a platform on your on-premises environment that generates millions of structured JSON text files totaling 100 GB daily. However, the on-premises environment cannot be accessed from the public internet. You want to explore and query the platform data using Google Cloud products. What is the recommended solution?
options: Use Cloud Scheduler to transfer the data daily to Cloud Storage, then import the data into BigQuery using the BigQuery Data Transfer Service.
Use a Transfer Appliance to transfer the data to Cloud Storage, then import the data into BigQuery using the BigQuery Data Transfer Service.
Use Transfer Service for on-premises data to transfer the data to Cloud Storage, then import the data into BigQuery using the BigQuery Data Transfer Service.
Use the BigQuery Data Transfer Service dataset copy feature to directly transfer all data into BigQuery.
answer: Use Transfer Service for on-premises data to transfer the data to Cloud Storage, then import the data into BigQuery using the BigQuery Data Transfer Service.
questions.explanation: The Transfer Service for on-premises data is designed specifically for securely transferring large volumes of data from on-premises environments to Google Cloud Storage. Since the on-premises environment cannot be accessed from the public internet, this service can be used to establish a secure connection and transfer the data. After transferring the data to Cloud Storage, the BigQuery Data Transfer Service can be used to import the data into BigQuery. This service provides an easy way to schedule and automate data imports from various sources into BigQuery. Option A involves using Cloud Scheduler to copy data from the on-premises environment to Cloud Storage, which may not be a feasible solution due to the lack of internet access. Option B involves using a Transfer Appliance, which may not be necessary for transferring 100 GB of data daily. Option D involves directly transferring all data into BigQuery, which may not be efficient or practical for large volumes of data

questions.question: How can you migrate a Redis database from an on-premises data center to a Memorystore for Redis instance while following Google-recommended practices and minimizing cost, time, and effort. Which option is the best?
options: Backup the Redis database as an RDB file, copy the file to a Cloud Storage bucket using the gsutil utility, and import the file into the Memorystore for Redis instance
Create a secondary instance of the Redis database on a Compute Engine instance, then perform a live cutover.
Use Dataflow to read the Redis database from the on-premises data center and write the data to a Memorystore for Redis instance
Write a shell script to migrate the Redis data and create a new Memorystore for Redis instance.
answer: Backup the Redis database as an RDB file, copy the file to a Cloud Storage bucket using the gsutil utility, and import the file into the Memorystore for Redis instance
questions.explanation: Option A is the best choice. The recommended practice for migrating a Redis database to Memorystore for Redis is to create an RDB backup of the database, copy the RDB file to Cloud Storage, and then import it into the Memorystore instance. This method is quick and easy to implement and provides minimal downtime. Option B is not recommended because it involves creating a secondary instance of the Redis database on a Compute Engine instance and performing a live cutover. This method can be complex and may result in data loss or extended downtime. Option C involves creating a Dataflow job to read the Redis database from the on-premises data center and write the data to a Memorystore for Redis instance. This method can be complex and may result in increased cost and time. Option D involves writing a shell script to migrate the Redis data and create a new Memorystore for Redis instance. This method can be error-prone and may result in data loss or extended downtime. Therefore, it is not a recommended approach.

questions.question: Your organization has 15 TB of data stored in a POSIX-compliant source in your on-premises data center that you need to transfer to Google Cloud. The data changes weekly and your network operations team has granted you 500 Mbps bandwidth to the public internet. You want to follow Google‘s recommended practices for a reliable weekly data transfer. What should you do?
options: Use Cloud Scheduler to schedule a weekly transfer of the data using gsutil with the -m parameter for optimal parallelism.
Use Transfer Appliance to transfer the data into a Google Kubernetes Engine cluster and then configure a weekly transfer job.
Install Storage Transfer Service agents on-premises in your data center and configure a weekly transfer job.
Install Storage Transfer Service on a Google Cloud virtual machine and configure a weekly transfer job.
answer: Install Storage Transfer Service agents on-premises in your data center and configure a weekly transfer job.
questions.explanation: Install Storage Transfer Service agents (for on-premises data in your data center) and then configure a weekly transfer job. Storage Transfer Service is a fully managed and cost-effective service that can handle transferring large amounts of data from on-premises data centers to Google Cloud. With the ability to configure a weekly transfer job, it provides a reliable and automated solution for regularly transferring large amounts of data over a limited bandwidth network The reason to install the agent in on-premise to have better performance. Ref: https://cloud.google.com/storage-transfer/docs/managing-on-prem-agents#file_system_transfer_details Ref: https://cloud.google.com/storage-transfer/docs/managing-on-prem-agents

questions.question: The company currently runs a large on-premises cluster using Spark, Hive, and HDFS and is planning to move to the cloud to benefit from cost savings and to modernize their infrastructure. They have only 2 months for their initial migration due to the timing of their contract renewal with the colocation facility. Which migration strategy should they adopt to maximize cost savings while completing the migration on time?
options: Migrate the workloads to Dataproc plus HDFS; modernize later: This option suggests moving the workloads to Dataproc plus HDFS without modernizing and doing so at a later time.
Migrate the workloads to Dataproc plus Cloud Storage; modernize later: This option suggests moving the workloads to Dataproc plus Cloud Storage without modernizing and doing so at a later time

Migrate the Spark workload to Dataproc plus HDFS and modernize the Hive workload for BigQuery: This option suggests migrating the Spark workload to Dataproc plus HDFS and modernizing the Hive workload for BigQuery
Modernize the Spark workload for Dataflow and the Hive workload for BigQuery: This option suggests modernizing the Spark workload for Dataflow and the Hive workload for BigQuery

Migrate the workloads to Dataproc plus Cloud Storage; modernize later: This option provides a good balance between speed and cost savings, allowing the company to meet its deadline while still achieving some cost optimization.
answer: Migrate the workloads to Dataproc plus Cloud Storage; modernize later: This option suggests moving the workloads to Dataproc plus Cloud Storage without modernizing and doing so at a later time

Migrate the workloads to Dataproc plus Cloud Storage; modernize later: This option provides a good balance between speed and cost savings, allowing the company to meet its deadline while still achieving some cost optimization.
questions.explanation: Correct Option B. Migrate the workloads to Dataproc plus Cloud Storage; modernize later. Correct because: Cloud Storage is a cost-effective and scalable replacement for HDFS. Migrating Spark and Hive workloads to Dataproc with Cloud Storage allows the company to quickly move off-premises, meet the tight deadline, and achieve immediate cost savings. Modernization (e.g., moving Hive to BigQuery or Spark to Dataflow) can be done later once the migration deadline is met. Option E. Migrate the workloads to Dataproc plus Cloud Storage; modernize later (balance between speed and cost savings). Correct because: This option essentially restates Option B but emphasizes the balance between speed and cost savings. It highlights why Cloud Storage is preferable over HDFS in the cloud migration scenario. Incorrect Option A. Migrate the workloads to Dataproc plus HDFS; modernize later. Incorrect because: Using HDFS in the cloud is not cost-efficient compared to Cloud Storage. It requires persistent VM clusters, which increases operational overhead and costs. This does not maximize cost savings. Option C. Migrate the Spark workload to Dataproc plus HDFS and modernize the Hive workload for BigQuery. Incorrect because: While modernizing Hive to BigQuery is beneficial long-term, doing so during the initial 2-month migration window adds complexity and risk. The tight deadline favors a simpler “lift-and-shift” approach first. Option D. Modernize the Spark workload for Dataflow and the Hive workload for BigQuery. Incorrect because: Full modernization within 2 months is unrealistic. Migrating and modernizing simultaneously would likely exceed the deadline and introduce unnecessary risk.

questions.question: You need to migrate 2 PB of historical data from an on-premises storage appliance to Cloud Storage within six months. Your outbound network capacity is limited to 20 Mb/sec. How should you perform this migration?
options: Use Transfer Appliance to copy the data to Cloud Storage
Compress the content using gsutil cp -J before uploading to Cloud Storage
Create a private URL for the historical data and use Storage Transfer Service to copy the data to Cloud Storage
Use a tool like trickle or ionice with gsutil cp to limit the bandwidth utilization to less than 20 Mb/sec to avoid interfering with production traffic
answer: Use Transfer Appliance to copy the data to Cloud Storage
questions.explanation: Correct Option A. Use Transfer Appliance to copy the data to Cloud Storage This is the correct answer. With 2 PB of data and only 20 Mb/sec outbound bandwidth, transferring over the network would take far longer than six months. Google’s Transfer Appliance is specifically designed for large-scale migrations, allowing data to be physically shipped to Google for upload into Cloud Storage. It is the most efficient, reliable, and cost-effective solution in this scenario. Incorrect Option B. Compress the content using gsutil cp -J before uploading Incorrect because compression may reduce data size but does not solve the bandwidth bottleneck. At 20 Mb/sec, even compressed data would take years to transfer 2 PB. Option C. Use Storage Transfer Service with a private URL Incorrect because Storage Transfer Service is designed for cloud-to-cloud transfers (e.g., AWS S3 to Cloud Storage). It cannot directly pull massive amounts of data from an on-premises appliance with limited bandwidth. Option D. Use trickle or ionice with gsutil cp to limit bandwidth Incorrect because throttling bandwidth only ensures production traffic is not disrupted. It does not address the fundamental issue: 20 Mb/sec is insufficient to transfer 2 PB in six months.

questions.question: You have the task of copying millions of sensitive patient records from a relational database with a total size of 10 TB to BigQuery. Your solution must be secure and time-efficient. Which option is the best solution?
options: Export the records from the database as an Avro file, upload the file to Google Cloud Storage (GCS) using gsutil, and then load the Avro file into BigQuery using the BigQuery web UI in the GCP Console

Export the records from the database as an Avro file, copy the file onto a Transfer Appliance, send it to Google, and then load the Avro file into BigQuery using the BigQuery web UI in the GCP Console.

Export the records from the database into a CSV file, create a public URL for the CSV file, use Storage Transfer Service to move the file to Cloud Storage, and then load the CSV file into BigQuery using the BigQuery web UI in the GCP Console.

Export the records from the database as an Avro file, create a public URL for the Avro file, use Storage Transfer Service to move the file to Cloud Storage, and then load the Avro file into BigQuery using the BigQuery web UI in the GCP Console.
answer: Export the records from the database as an Avro file, copy the file onto a Transfer Appliance, send it to Google, and then load the Avro file into BigQuery using the BigQuery web UI in the GCP Console.
questions.explanation: Correct Option B: Export the records from the database as an Avro file, copy the file onto a Transfer Appliance, send it to Google, and then load the Avro file into BigQuery using the BigQuery web UI in the GCP Console. For large datasets (10 TB), Google’s Transfer Appliance is the recommended solution. It provides a secure, offline transfer method that avoids long and unreliable network uploads. Avro is efficient, splittable, and natively supported by BigQuery, making it ideal for bulk ingestion. This approach balances security (no public URLs, encrypted appliance) and time efficiency (avoids slow uploads). Incorrect Option A: Export the records from the database as an Avro file, upload the file to Google Cloud Storage (GCS) using gsutil, and then load the Avro file into BigQuery using the BigQuery web UI in the GCP Console. Uploading 10 TB via gsutil over the network is time-consuming and prone to interruptions. While technically possible, it is not efficient for such a large dataset. Option C: Export the records from the database into a CSV file, create a public URL for the CSV file, use Storage Transfer Service to move the file to Cloud Storage, and then load the CSV file into BigQuery using the BigQuery web UI in the GCP Console. CSV is inefficient for large-scale data compared to Avro or Parquet. Creating a public URL for sensitive patient records is a security risk and violates compliance requirements. Not suitable for secure healthcare data migration. Option D: Export the records from the database as an Avro file, create a public URL for the Avro file, use Storage Transfer Service to move the file to Cloud Storage, and then load the Avro file into BigQuery using the BigQuery web UI in the GCP Console. Although Avro is efficient, exposing sensitive patient data via a public URL is a serious security violation. This fails the requirement for secure transfer.

questions.question: Can you recommend tools for migrating a data warehouse to Google Cloud and ensuring real-time updates to the warehouse from transactional systems. The files being transferred are not large in number but are each 90 GB in size?
options: Use Storage Transfer Service for the migration; Pub/Sub and Cloud Data Fusion for real-time updates
Use BigQuery Data Transfer Service for the migration; Pub/Sub and Dataproc for real-time updates
Use gsutil for the migration; Pub/Sub and Dataflow for real-time updates
Use gsutil for both the migration and real-time updates
answer: Use gsutil for the migration; Pub/Sub and Dataflow for real-time updates
questions.explanation: The gsutil tool is the standard tool for small- to medium-sized transfers (less than 1 TB) over a typical enterprise-scale network, from a private data center to Google Cloud. Use Gsutil when there is enough bandwidth to meet your project deadline for less than 1 TB of data. Storage Transfer Service is for much larger volumes for migration. Moreover, Cloud Data Fusion and Dataproc are not ideal for real-time updates. BigQuery Data Transfer Service does not support all on-prem sources. https://cloud.google.com/architecture/migration-to-google-cloud-transferring-your-large-datasets#gsutil_for_smaller_transfers_of_on-premises_data

questions.question: You are tasked with migrating 1 PB of data from an on-premises data center to Google Cloud, with the goal of minimizing transfer time and following Google-recommended best practices for a secure connection. Which option should you choose?
options: Use the Storage Transfer Service after establishing a Cloud Interconnect connection between the on-premises data center and Google Cloud
Use a Transfer Appliance and have engineers manually encrypt, decrypt, and verify the data
Establish a Cloud VPN connection, transfer the data using gcloud compute scp jobs in parallel, and run checksums to verify the data.
Transfer the data using gsutil in 3 TB batches, and run checksums to verify the data.
answer: Use the Storage Transfer Service after establishing a Cloud Interconnect connection between the on-premises data center and Google Cloud
questions.explanation: To transfer a large amount of data (1 PB) within a few hours, it is recommended to use a dedicated network connection like Cloud Interconnect, which can provide higher bandwidth and lower latency compared to using the public internet. The Storage Transfer Service can be used to transfer data between on-premises and cloud storage, and supports Cloud Interconnect. This approach ensures that the data transfer is fast, secure, and reliable. Option B, using a Transfer Appliance, requires manual handling of the data and can take longer. Option C, using gcloud compute scp jobs, can also take longer and may not be as reliable. Option D, reducing the data into smaller batches, can increase the complexity of the migration process and may not meet the requirement to transfer the data within a few hours

questions.question: You need to copy millions of sensitive patient records from a relational database to BigQuery. The total size of the database is 10 TB. You need to design a solution that is secure and time-efficient. What should you do?
options: Export the records from the database as an Avro file. Upload the file to GCS using gsutil, and then load the Avro file into BigQuery using the BigQuery web UI in the GCP Console.

Export the records from the database as an Avro file. Copy the file onto a Transfer Appliance and send it to Google, and then load the Avro file into BigQuery using the BigQuery web UI in the GCP Console.

Export the records from the database into a CSV file. Create a public URL for the CSV file, and then use Storage Transfer Service to move the file to Cloud Storage. Load the CSV file into BigQuery using the BigQuery web UI in the GCP Console.

Export the records from the database as an Avro file. Create a public URL for the Avro file, and then use Storage Transfer Service to move the file to Cloud Storage. Load the Avro file into BigQuery using the BigQuery web UI in the GCP Console.
answer: Export the records from the database as an Avro file. Copy the file onto a Transfer Appliance and send it to Google, and then load the Avro file into BigQuery using the BigQuery web UI in the GCP Console.
questions.explanation: Correct Option B: Export the records from the database as an Avro file. Copy the file onto a Transfer Appliance and send it to Google, and then load the Avro file into BigQuery using the BigQuery web UI in the GCP Console. Why it is correct: * Transfer Appliance: For 10 TB of data, a physical Transfer Appliance is often more time-efficient than an online transfer if bandwidth is limited. More importantly, it is highly secure for sensitive data as it avoids the public internet entirely during the primary transit phase. Avro Format: Avro is the recommended format for BigQuery loading because it is a binary format with an embedded schema, making it faster to process and less prone to errors than CSV. Workflow: Physical transit to Google, followed by a direct load into BigQuery, is a standard large-scale migration pattern. Incorrect Option A: Export as Avro and upload using gsutil. Why it is incorrect: While Avro is a good choice, gsutil relies on your local internet connection. Uploading 10 TB over a typical corporate line (e.g., 100 Mbps) would take weeks, which fails the “time-efficient” requirement compared to a physical appliance. Option C & D: Create a public URL and use Storage Transfer Service. Why they are incorrect: These options are massive security violations. You must never create a “public URL” for sensitive patient records. Even if the data is encrypted, exposing a URL to the public internet violates most compliance standards (like HIPAA) and the “secure” requirement of the prompt. CSV vs Avro (Option C): CSV is also less efficient than Avro for 10 TB of data because BigQuery cannot parse it as quickly and it lacks built-in schema definitions.

questions.question: Your company‘s on-premises Apache Hadoop servers are approaching end-of-life, and IT has decided to migrate the cluster to Google Cloud Dataproc. A like-for- like migration of the cluster would require 50 TB of Google Persistent Disk per node. The CIO is concerned about the cost of using that much block storage. You want to minimize the storage cost of the migration. What should you do?
options: Put the data into Google Cloud Storage.
Use preemptible virtual machines (VMs) for the Cloud Dataproc cluster.
Tune the Cloud Dataproc cluster so that there is just enough disk for all data.
Migrate some of the cold data into Google Cloud Storage, and keep only the hot data in Persistent Disk.
answer: Put the data into Google Cloud Storage.
questions.explanation: Ans: A B: Wrong eVM wont solve the problem of larger storage prices. C: May be, but nothing mentioned in terms of what to tune in the question, also this is like-for-like migration so tuning may not be part of the migration. D: Again, this is like-for-like so need to define what is hot data and which is cold data, also persistent disk costlier than cloud storage.

questions.question: You want to migrate an on-premises Hadoop system to Cloud Dataproc. Hive is the primary tool in use, and the data format is Optimized Row Columnar (ORC).All ORC files have been successfully copied to a Cloud Storage bucket. You need to replicate some data to the cluster‘s local Hadoop Distributed File System(HDFS) to maximize performance. What are two ways to start using Hive in Cloud Dataproc? (Choose two.)
options: Run the gsutil utility to transfer all ORC files from the Cloud Storage bucket to HDFS. Mount the Hive tables locally.
Run the gsutil utility to transfer all ORC files from the Cloud Storage bucket to any node of the Dataproc cluster. Mount the Hive tables locally.

Run the gsutil utility to transfer all ORC files from the Cloud Storage bucket to the master node of the Dataproc cluster. Then run the Hadoop utility to copy them do HDFS. Mount the Hive tables from HDFS.
Leverage Cloud Storage connector for Hadoop to mount the ORC files as external Hive tables. Replicate external Hive tables to the native ones.
Load the ORC files into BigQuery. Leverage BigQuery connector for Hadoop to mount the BigQuery tables as external Hive tables. Replicate external Hive tables to the native ones.
answer: Run the gsutil utility to transfer all ORC files from the Cloud Storage bucket to the master node of the Dataproc cluster. Then run the Hadoop utility to copy them do HDFS. Mount the Hive tables from HDFS.
Leverage Cloud Storage connector for Hadoop to mount the ORC files as external Hive tables. Replicate external Hive tables to the native ones.
questions.explanation: C. Run the gsutil utility to transfer some ORC files from the Cloud Storage bucket to the master node of the Dataproc cluster. Then run the Hadoop utility to copy them do HDFS. Mount the Hive tables from HDFS. This method leverages a combination of tools: * **gsutil**: The Google Cloud Storage command-line tool to transfer a subset of ORC files from Cloud Storage to the master node of your Dataproc cluster. Transferring only a subset initially can help assess performance before potentially moving the entire dataset. * **Hadoop utilities**: Once on the master node, you can utilize Hadoop utilities like ′distcp′ to copy the ORC files from the local node storage to the cluster‘s HDFS. * **Hive table mounting**: After the data resides in HDFS, you can leverage Hive capabilities to mount these tables, allowing you to interact with the data using HiveQL queries. D. Leverage Cloud Storage connector for Hadoop to mount the ORC files as external Hive tables. Replicate external Hive tables to the native ones. This approach utilizes the Cloud Storage connector for Hadoop: * **Cloud Storage connector**: This connector allows Hive to access data directly from Cloud Storage without physically moving the data to HDFS. * **External Hive tables**: By mounting the ORC files in Cloud Storage as external Hive tables, you can still query and interact with the data using HiveQL. * **Replication (optional)**: While using external Hive tables provides access to the data, for performance-critical workloads, you might consider replicating a subset of the data to HDFS for faster query execution. This can be done later as needed. Other options and why they are less suitable: A. Transferring all ORC files and mounting locally: While technically possible, transferring the entire dataset to all nodes might not be necessary, especially for initial exploration. Additionally, mounting tables locally might not be the most efficient approach for a distributed cluster. B. Transferring ORC files to any node: While it might work, transferring data to any node is less controlled compared to using the master node as a designated entry point. E. Loading into BigQuery: While BigQuery is a powerful tool, it might be an unnecessary step if the primary goal is to use Hive for data management and querying within the Cloud Dataproc environment.

questions.question: Your company produces 20,000 files every hour. Each data file is formatted as a comma separated values (CSV) file that is less than 4 KB. All files must be ingested on Google Cloud Platform before they can be processed. Your company site has a 200 ms latency to Google Cloud, and your Internet connection bandwidth is limited as 50 Mbps. You currently deploy a secure FTP (SFTP) server on a virtual machine in Google Compute Engine as the data ingestion point. A local SFTP client runs on a dedicated machine to transmit the CSV files as is. The goal is to make reports with data from the previous day available to the executives by 10:00 a.m. each day. This design is barely able to keep up with the current volume, even though the bandwidth utilization is rather low.You are told that due to seasonality, your company expects the number of files to double for the next three months. Which two actions should you take? (Choose two.)
options: Introduce data compression for each file to increase the rate file of file transfer.
Contact your internet service provider (ISP) to increase your maximum bandwidth to at least 100 Mbps.
Redesign the data ingestion process to use gsutil tool to send the CSV files to a storage bucket in parallel.
Assemble 1,000 files into a tape archive (TAR) file. Transmit the TAR files instead, and disassemble the CSV files in the cloud upon receiving them.
Create an S3-compatible storage endpoint in your network, and use Google Cloud Storage Transfer Service to transfer on-premises data to the designated storage bucket.
answer: Introduce data compression for each file to increase the rate file of file transfer.
Redesign the data ingestion process to use gsutil tool to send the CSV files to a storage bucket in parallel.
questions.explanation: I’d recommend two actions to address the data ingestion bottleneck and ensure timely report generation: A. Introduce data compression for each file to increase the rate of file transfer. C. Redesign the data ingestion process to use gsutil tool to send the CSV files to a storage bucket in parallel. Here’s why these options are the most suitable: Data Compression (A): Compressing individual CSV files can significantly reduce their size, leading to faster transfers even with limited bandwidth (50 Mbps). This is because you’re transmitting less data overall. Compression techniques like Gzip are well-suited for CSV files. Parallel Ingestion with gsutil (C): gsutil is a command-line tool for interacting with Google Cloud Storage. By redesigning the process to use gsutil, you can upload files in parallel. This leverages multiple connections to the cloud, improving overall throughput compared to a single SFTP connection. Why other options are less effective: B. Increase Bandwidth (Not as critical): While increasing bandwidth to 100 Mbps could help, it might not be the most cost-effective solution, especially if compression significantly reduces file sizes. D. TAR Archiving (Less efficient): While TAR archiving can group files, it adds an extra processing step (decompression) in the cloud, potentially negating any transfer speed benefits. Individual compression is more efficient. E. S3 Storage Endpoint (Not applicable): Google Cloud Storage is the recommended storage solution within GCP. Transfer Service is designed for moving data between on-premises storage and GCP buckets. Since you’re already in GCP, using gsutil for direct upload is simpler. By implementing data compression and parallel ingestion with gsutil, you can efficiently handle the current and anticipated data volume (20,000 files/hour doubling for 3 months) and ensure reports are available by 10:00 AM daily.

questions.question: You need to move 2 PB of historical data from an on-premises storage appliance to Cloud Storage within six months, and your outbound network capacity is constrained to 20 Mb/sec. How should you migrate this data to Cloud Storage?
options: Use Transfer Appliance to copy the data to Cloud Storage
Use gsutil cp ““J to compress the content being uploaded to Cloud Storage
Create a private URL for the historical data, and then use Storage Transfer Service to copy the data to Cloud Storage
Use trickle or ionice along with gsutil cp to limit the amount of bandwidth gsutil utilizes to less than 20 Mb/sec so it does not interfere with the production traffic
answer: Use Transfer Appliance to copy the data to Cloud Storage
questions.explanation: Vote for A A – Correct , Transfer Appliance for moving offline data, large data sets, or data from a source with limited bandwidth https://cloud.google.com/storage-transfer/docs/overview B – Eliminated (Not recommended for large storage). recommended for < 1TB C - Its ONLINE, but we have bandwidth issue - So eliminated. D - Eliminated (Not recommended for large storage). recommended for < 1TB

questions.question: A data engineer needs to load data stored in Avro files in Cloud Storage into Bigtable. They would like to have a reliable, easily monitored process for copying the data. What would you recommend they use to copy the data?
options: Custom Python 3 program
Storage Transfer Service
Cloud Dataflow, starting with a Cloud Storage Avro to Bigtable template.
gsutil
answer: Cloud Dataflow, starting with a Cloud Storage Avro to Bigtable template.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is to use Cloud Dataflow with a Cloud Storage Avro to Bigtable template. Using Python 3 would require more work than necessary. Gsutil is used to load data into Cloud Storage, not Bigtable. Storage Transfer Service is for copying data into Cloud Storage from other object storage system, such as AWS S3. A custom Python 3 program would require more development effort than using Cloud Dataflow. See https://cloud.google.com/architecture/streaming-avro-records-into-bigquery-using-dataflow

questions.question: How can you design a solution to transfer log data from your set of YouTube channels into Google Cloud for analysis. Your design should allow your global marketing teams to perform ANSI SQL and other types of analysis on the latest YouTube channel log data.?
options: Use BigQuery Data Transfer Service to transfer the offsite backup files to a Cloud Storage Regional storage bucket as a final destination
Use Storage Transfer Service to transfer the offsite backup files to a Cloud Storage Multi-Regional storage bucket as a final destination
Use Storage Transfer Service to transfer the offsite backup files to a Cloud Storage Regional bucket as a final destination.
Use BigQuery Data Transfer Service to transfer the offsite backup files to a Cloud Storage Multi-Regional storage bucket as a final destination.
answer: Use Storage Transfer Service to transfer the offsite backup files to a Cloud Storage Multi-Regional storage bucket as a final destination
questions.explanation: Correct Option B. Use Storage Transfer Service to transfer the offsite backup files to a Cloud Storage Multi‑Regional storage bucket as a final destination Correct because: Storage Transfer Service is designed to move large datasets (including logs/backups) into Cloud Storage. Using a Multi‑Regional bucket ensures global availability and durability, which is ideal for marketing teams distributed worldwide. Once in Cloud Storage, the data can be loaded into BigQuery for ANSI SQL analysis and other advanced analytics. This option aligns with the exam scenario: scalable ingestion, global accessibility, and SQL‑based analysis. Incorrect Option A. Use BigQuery Data Transfer Service to transfer the offsite backup files to a Cloud Storage Regional storage bucket Incorrect because BigQuery Data Transfer Service is for specific SaaS connectors (Google Ads, YouTube Analytics, etc.), not for moving raw backup files into Cloud Storage. Also, a Regional bucket limits global accessibility compared to Multi‑Regional. Option C. Use Storage Transfer Service to transfer the offsite backup files to a Cloud Storage Regional bucket Incorrect because while Storage Transfer Service is correct, using a Regional bucket does not meet the requirement for global marketing teams. Regional storage is optimized for local workloads, not global distribution. Option D. Use BigQuery Data Transfer Service to transfer the offsite backup files to a Cloud Storage Multi‑Regional bucket Incorrect because BigQuery Data Transfer Service does not transfer raw backup files into Cloud Storage. It is intended for direct SaaS‑to‑BigQuery transfers, not Cloud Storage ingestion.

questions.question: Scenario: You are transferring a large number of files from a public HTTPS endpoint to Cloud Storage. The files are secured with signed URLs to prevent unauthorized access. After creating a TSV file containing the object URLs, you initiated a transfer job using Storage Transfer Service. However, the job failed after running for a considerable time. Upon reviewing the logs, you discovered that the job was progressing smoothly until it encountered HTTP 403 errors on the remaining files. You confirmed that there were no modifications made to the source system. What steps should you take to address the issue and continue the migration process smoothly?
options: Set up Cloud Storage FUSE, and mount the Cloud Storage bucket on a Compute Engine instance. Remove the completed files from the TSV file. Use a shell script to iterate through the TSV file and download the remaining URLs to the FUSE mount point.
Renew the TLS certificate of the HTTPS endpoint. Remove the completed files from the TSV file and rerun the Storage Transfer Service job.

Create a new TSV file for the remaining files by generating signed URLs with a longer validity period. Split the TSV file into multiple smaller files and submit them as separate Storage Transfer Service jobs in parallel.
Update the file checksums in the TSV file from using MD5 to SHA256. Remove the completed files from the TSV file and rerun the Storage Transfer Service job.
answer: Create a new TSV file for the remaining files by generating signed URLs with a longer validity period. Split the TSV file into multiple smaller files and submit them as separate Storage Transfer Service jobs in parallel.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is C.– The issue of the HTTP 403 errors on the remaining files is likely due to the expiration of the signed URLs used for accessing the files from the public HTTPS endpoint. Signed URLs have an expiration time after which they become invalid for access.– By creating a new TSV file for the remaining files and generating signed URLs with a longer validity period, you ensure that the URLs will remain valid for a longer time, allowing the Storage Transfer Service job to complete without encountering 403 errors.Why the other options are incorrect:A. Setting up Cloud Storage FUSE and mounting the bucket on a Compute Engine instance is not necessary and does not address the issue of HTTP 403 errors due to expired signed URLs.B. Renewing the TLS certificate of the HTTPS endpoint does not address the issue of signed URLs expiration and would not fix the problem of HTTP 403 errors on the remaining files.D. Updating the file checksums in the TSV file from MD5 to SHA256 does not address the issue of HTTP 403 errors due to expired signed URLs. This option focuses on file integrity verification but does not solve the main problem of URL expiration.

questions.question: Scenario: You have an upstream process that writes data to Cloud Storage. This data is then read by an Apache Spark job that runs on Dataproc. These jobs are run in the us-central1 region, but the data could be stored anywhere in the United States. You need to have a recovery process in place in case of a catastrophic single region failure. What steps should be taken to ensure a maximum of 15 minutes of data loss (RPO=15 mins) and minimal latency when reading the data?
options: 1. Create two regional Cloud Storage buckets, one in the us-central1 region and one in the us-south1 region. 2. Have the upstream process write data to the us-central1 bucket. Use the Storage Transfer Service to copy data hourly from the us-central1 bucket to the us-south1 bucket. 3. Run the Dataproc cluster in a zone in the us-central1 region, reading from the bucket in that region. 4. In case of regional failure, redeploy your Dataproc clusters to the us-south1 region and read from the bucket in that region instead.

1. Create a Cloud Storage bucket in the US multi-region. 2. Run the Dataproc cluster in a zone in the us-central1 region, reading data from the US multi-region bucket. 3. In case of a regional failure, redeploy the Dataproc cluster to the us-central2 region and continue reading from the same bucket.

1. Create a dual-region Cloud Storage bucket in the us-central1 and us-south1 regions. 2. Enable turbo replication. 3. Run the Dataproc cluster in a zone in the us-central1 region, reading from the bucket in the us-south1 region. 4. In case of a regional failure, redeploy your Dataproc cluster to the us-south1 region and continue reading from the same bucket.

1. Create a dual-region Cloud Storage bucket in the us-central1 and us-south1 regions. 2. Enable turbo replication. 3. Run the Dataproc cluster in a zone in the us-central1 region, reading from the bucket in the same region. 4. In case of a regional failure, redeploy the Dataproc clusters to the us-south1 region and read from the same bucket.
answer: 1. Create a dual-region Cloud Storage bucket in the us-central1 and us-south1 regions. 2. Enable turbo replication. 3. Run the Dataproc cluster in a zone in the us-central1 region, reading from the bucket in the same region. 4. In case of a regional failure, redeploy the Dataproc clusters to the us-south1 region and read from the same bucket.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option D:1. Create a dual-region Cloud Storage bucket in the us-central1 and us-south1 regions.2. Enable turbo replication.3. Run the Dataproc cluster in a zone in the us-central1 region, reading from the bucket in the same region.4. In case of a regional failure, redeploy the Dataproc clusters to the us-south1 region and read from the same bucket.– Creating a dual-region Cloud Storage bucket in both the us-central1 and us-south1 regions ensures data redundancy and availability in case of a catastrophic single region failure.– Enabling turbo replication ensures that data is replicated quickly between the two regions, minimizing data loss.– Running the Dataproc cluster in the us-central1 region provides low latency for reading the data.– In case of a regional failure, redeploying the Dataproc cluster to the us-south1 region allows for continued access to the data stored in the dual-region bucket with minimal disruption.Why the other options are incorrect:– Option A suggests using regional buckets and hourly data transfer which may not meet the RPO requirement of 15 minutes.– Option B suggests using a US multi-region bucket, which may not provide the same level of redundancy as a dual-region bucket in case of a catastrophic single region failure.– Option C suggests using turbo replication but reading from a bucket in a different region, which may introduce latency when reading the data. Also, redeploying the Dataproc cluster to a different region may not be efficient in case of a regional failure.

questions.question: Scenario: You are creating a fault-tolerant architecture for storing data in a regional BigQuery dataset. Your goal is to enable your application to recover from a corruption event in your tables that happened in the last seven days. What steps should you take to implement a managed service solution with the lowest RPO and cost-effectiveness?
options: Access historical data by using time travel in BigQuery.
Export the data from BigQuery into a new table that excludes the corrupted data
Create a BigQuery table snapshot on a daily basis.
Migrate your data to multi-region BigQuery buckets.
answer: Access historical data by using time travel in BigQuery.
questions.explanation: A. Access historical data by using time travel in BigQuery.– Time travel in BigQuery allows you to query data stored in a table at any point in the past within the past seven days. This feature is helpful in recovering from a corruption event as you can access the historical data before the corruption occurred.– Time travel in BigQuery provides a low Recovery Point Objective (RPO) because you can query the data as it was before the corruption event.– Using time travel in BigQuery is a managed service provided by Google Cloud, making it a cost-effective solution compared to other options.Why other options are incorrect:B. Export the data from BigQuery into a new table that excludes the corrupted data:– Exporting data and creating a new table without the corrupted data is not a fault-tolerant solution as the corruption might still exist in the original table.– This option does not provide a way to recover the data as it was before the corruption event.C. Create a BigQuery table snapshot on a daily basis:– Creating table snapshots on a daily basis can be a good practice for backup purposes, but it does not provide a way to recover data from a corruption event within the past seven days.– This option does not offer the ability to access historical data as it was before the corruption occurred.D. Migrate your data to multi-region BigQuery buckets:– Migrating data to multi-region buckets can help with data redundancy and availability but does not directly address the requirement to recover from a corruption event within the past seven days.– This option does not provide a way to access historical data for recovery purposes.

questions.question: Scenario: To migrate a Redis database from an on-premises data center to a Memorystore for Redis instance while adhering to Google-recommended practices and ensuring minimal cost, time, and effort. What steps should be taken to achieve this migration effectively and efficiently?
options: Make an RDB backup of the Redis database, use the gsutil utility to copy the RDB file into a Cloud Storage bucket, and then import the RDB file into the Memorystore for Redis instance.
Make a secondary instance of the Redis database on a Compute Engine instance and then perform a live cutover.
Create a Dataflow job to read the Redis database from the on-premises data center and write the data to a Memorystore for Redis instance.
Write a shell script to migrate the Redis data and create a new Memorystore for Redis instance.
answer: Make an RDB backup of the Redis database, use the gsutil utility to copy the RDB file into a Cloud Storage bucket, and then import the RDB file into the Memorystore for Redis instance.
questions.explanation: A. Make an RDB backup of the Redis database, use the gsutil utility to copy the RDB file into a Cloud Storage bucket, and then import the RDB file into the Memorystore for Redis instance.– Making an RDB backup of the Redis database ensures that the data is backed up in a suitable format for migration.– Using the gsutil utility to copy the RDB file into a Cloud Storage bucket is a recommended practice for securely transferring the data to Google Cloud Platform.– Importing the RDB file into the Memorystore for Redis instance allows for a straightforward and efficient migration process.– This approach aligns with Google-recommended practices and minimizes cost, time, and effort.Why the other options are incorrect:B. Making a secondary instance of the Redis database on a Compute Engine instance and performing a live cutover is not a recommended practice for migrating to Memorystore for Redis. This approach involves additional complexity and does not align with the goal of minimal cost, time, and effort.C. Creating a Dataflow job to read the Redis database from the on-premises data center and write the data to a Memorystore for Redis instance introduces unnecessary complexity and cost. This method is not the most efficient way to migrate a Redis database to Memorystore.D. Writing a shell script to migrate the Redis data and create a new Memorystore for Redis instance might work, but it is not the most efficient or recommended approach. Using manual scripts for migration may lead to errors and is not as straightforward as using built-in tools like gsutil for data transfer.

questions.question: Scenario: You are tasked with transferring 1 PB of data from an on-premises data center to Google Cloud within a few hours while following Google-recommended practices for a secure connection. What steps should be taken to facilitate the secure and efficient transfer of such a large amount of data to Google Cloud within a short timeframe?
options: Establish a Cloud Interconnect connection between the on-premises data center and Google Cloud, and then use the Storage Transfer Service.
Use a Transfer Appliance and have engineers manually encrypt, decrypt, and verify the data.
Establish a Cloud VPN connection, start gcloud compute scp jobs in parallel, and run checksums to verify the data.
Reduce the data into 3 TB batches, transfer the data using gsutil, and run checksums to verify the data.
answer: Establish a Cloud Interconnect connection between the on-premises data center and Google Cloud, and then use the Storage Transfer Service.
questions.explanation: A. Establish a Cloud Interconnect connection between the on-premises data center and Google Cloud, and then use the Storage Transfer Service.– Cloud Interconnect provides a dedicated, high-throughput, low-latency connection between on-premises networks and Google Cloud. This ensures a secure and efficient data transfer.– Storage Transfer Service is a Google Cloud service designed for transferring large amounts of data from on-premises to Google Cloud. It automates and simplifies the data transfer process.– By combining Cloud Interconnect with Storage Transfer Service, the data transfer can be done quickly and securely, meeting the requirement of completing the migration in a few hours.Why the other options are incorrect:B. Using a Transfer Appliance and having engineers manually encrypt, decrypt, and verify the data would be time-consuming and not efficient for transferring a large amount of data in just a few hours.C. Establishing a Cloud VPN connection and running gcloud compute scp jobs in parallel with checksums would not provide the same level of performance and security as using Cloud Interconnect and Storage Transfer Service.D. Reducing the data into 3 TB batches and transferring with gsutil may not meet the requirement of completing the migration in a few hours, especially for a large dataset of 1 PB. Additionally, running checksums for each batch could add extra time to the migration process.

questions.question: Scenario: You have 15 TB of data in your on-premises data center that needs to be transferred to Google Cloud. The data changes weekly and is stored in a POSIX-compliant source. Your network operations team has provided you with 500 Mbps bandwidth to the public internet. How can you follow Google-recommended practices to reliably transfer your 15 TB of data to Google Cloud on a weekly basis?
options: Use Cloud Scheduler to trigger the gsutil command. Use the -m parameter for optimal parallelism.
Use Transfer Appliance to migrate your data into a Google Kubernetes Engine cluster, and then configure a weekly transfer job.
Install Storage Transfer Service for on-premises data in your data center, and then configure a weekly transfer job.
Install Storage Transfer Service for on-premises data on a Google Cloud virtual machine, and then configure a weekly transfer job.
answer: Install Storage Transfer Service for on-premises data in your data center, and then configure a weekly transfer job.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is C. Install Storage Transfer Service for on-premises data in your data center, and then configure a weekly transfer job.Using Storage Transfer Service for on-premises data in your data center is the recommended approach for transferring data from an on-premises environment to Google Cloud. This service provides a reliable and efficient way to transfer data to Google Cloud Storage on a scheduled basis. By setting up a weekly transfer job, you can ensure that your data changes are transferred to Google Cloud regularly and automatically.Why the other options are incorrect:A. Using Cloud Scheduler to trigger the gsutil command with the -m parameter for optimal parallelism is not the best approach for transferring large amounts of data on a weekly basis. While gsutil supports parallel transfers, it may not be the most efficient method for transferring 15 TB of data in a timely manner.B. Using Transfer Appliance to migrate your data into a Google Kubernetes Engine cluster and configuring a weekly transfer job is not necessary in this scenario. Transfer Appliance is typically used for large-scale data migrations where shipping physical storage devices is more efficient than transferring data over the internet. In this case, with a 500 Mbps bandwidth to the public internet, using Transfer Appliance would not be the most cost-effective or efficient solution.D. Installing Storage Transfer Service for on-premises data on a Google Cloud virtual machine is not the best practice for transferring data from an on-premises data center to Google Cloud. The recommended approach is to install the Storage Transfer Service in your on-premises data center to facilitate the transfer of data to Google Cloud Storage directly. Setting up a virtual machine in Google Cloud would introduce unnecessary complexity and additional costs.

questions.question: Scenario: Your on-premises environment generates 100 GB of data daily, consisting of millions of structured JSON text files. This environment is not accessible from the public internet. How can you leverage Google Cloud products to query and explore the data from your on-premises platform?
options: Use Cloud Scheduler to copy data daily from your on-premises environment to Cloud Storage. Use the BigQuery Data Transfer Service to import data into BigQuery.
Use a Transfer Appliance to copy data from your on-premises environment to Cloud Storage. Use the BigQuery Data Transfer Service to import data into BigQuery.
Use Transfer Service for on-premises data to copy data from your on-premises environment to Cloud Storage. Use the BigQuery Data Transfer Service to import data into BigQuery.
Use the BigQuery Data Transfer Service dataset copy to transfer all data into BigQuery.
answer: Use Transfer Service for on-premises data to copy data from your on-premises environment to Cloud Storage. Use the BigQuery Data Transfer Service to import data into BigQuery.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is C: Use Transfer Service for on-premises data to copy data from your on-premises environment to Cloud Storage. Use the BigQuery Data Transfer Service to import data into BigQuery.1. Transfer Service for on-premises data allows you to securely transfer data from your on-premises environment to Google Cloud Storage. Since your on-premises environment cannot be accessed from the public internet, this option provides a secure way to transfer the data.2. Once the data is transferred to Cloud Storage, you can use the BigQuery Data Transfer Service to import the data into BigQuery for querying and exploration. BigQuery Data Transfer Service simplifies data movement into BigQuery, making it a suitable choice for importing and analyzing large amounts of data.Now, let‘s explain why the other options are incorrect:A. Using Cloud Scheduler to copy data daily from your on-premises environment to Cloud Storage is not feasible in this scenario because your on-premises environment cannot be accessed from the public internet. Cloud Scheduler requires internet access to trigger the data transfer.B. Using a Transfer Appliance to copy data from your on-premises environment to Cloud Storage is not suitable because Transfer Appliances are physical devices that need to be shipped to you. Since your on-premises environment cannot be accessed from the public internet, using a Transfer Appliance would not be practical.D. Using the BigQuery Data Transfer Service dataset copy to transfer all data into BigQuery is not the best option because it does not address the initial step of transferring data from your on-premises environment to Cloud Storage. This option assumes the data is already available in Cloud Storage, which is not the case in this scenario.

questions.question: Scenario: You have 2 PB of historical data stored in an on-premises storage appliance that needs to be moved to Cloud Storage within six months. Your outbound network capacity is limited to 20 Mb/sec. What is the best approach to migrate this data to Cloud Storage considering the network constraints and timeframe?
options: Use Transfer Appliance to copy the data to Cloud Storage
Use gsutil cp ?“J to compress the content being uploaded to Cloud Storage
Create a private URL for the historical data, and then use Storage Transfer Service to copy the data to Cloud Storage
Use trickle or ionice along with gsutil cp to limit the amount of bandwidth gsutil utilizes to less than 20 Mb/sec so it does not interfere with the production traffic
answer: Use Transfer Appliance to copy the data to Cloud Storage
questions.explanation: A. Use Transfer Appliance to copy the data to Cloud Storage.Using Transfer Appliance is the best option for moving 2 PB of historical data within six months when the outbound network capacity is constrained to 20 Mb/sec. Transfer Appliance is a physical storage appliance provided by Google Cloud that allows you to securely transfer large amounts of data to Google Cloud Storage. You can load your data onto the appliance and then ship it to a Google Cloud data center for fast and secure data transfer.Why the other options are incorrect:B. Using gsutil cp with compression: While compression can help reduce the size of the data being transferred, it won‘t address the network capacity constraint. The compression process may also add extra processing overhead and time, which could be counterproductive in this scenario.C. Creating a private URL and using Storage Transfer Service: This option involves transferring the data over the network, which is limited to 20 Mb/sec. It does not provide a solution to overcome the network capacity constraint within the required timeframe.D. Using trickle or ionice with gsutil cp: While these tools can help limit the bandwidth utilization of gsutil, they may not be as effective in ensuring the timely transfer of 2 PB of data within six months. Additionally, managing bandwidth using these tools may require more manual intervention and monitoring compared to using Transfer Appliance.
